title: Jayavarman VIII — Iconoclasm, Kingship, and Angkor’s Last Stone - Varro

The Buddhist images that had softened Angkor’s gaze were altered, removed, or erased. At the Bayon, the Buddha of the central sanctuary was broken and cast down. Yet even this act feels less like rage than anxiety: a ruler attempting to  ...    Jayavarman VIII was not merely undoing his predecessors; he was trying to re-tighten the mandala. His building works tell the same story. The expansions of the Terrace of the Leper King, the continued use of older Hindu sites, the dedication of East Top Temple—also known as Mangalartha—in 1295: these were not gestures of ambition, but punctuation marks. title: Khmer's Last Push for Glory | What an Amazing World!

Among the many apsaras at Bayon  ...    VII’s temples. In 1243, a new king emerged. Taking the title Jayavarman VIII, the Shaivite king embarked on a campaign to restore Hinduism as the state religion. He did so by converting Bayon into a Hindu temple, a chapter in the Khmer history when Hindu iconography was added to the inner gallery of the former Buddhist state temple, as well as by defacing images of the Buddha at the temples Jayavarman VII built.
